book_claims.claim: Despite high glycaemic index, very low carbohydrate load makes them healthy
book_claims.mechanism: High GI but very low total carbohydrate content means negligible actual glucose load; provides vitamins and phytochemicals
book_claims.study_ref: book-assertion (glycaemic index vs glucose load distinction)

book_claims.claim: Loaded with carotenoids essential for vitamin A production, eye health, immune function and prenatal development
book_claims.mechanism: Beta-carotene is converted to vitamin A which plays key role in eye and immune function

book_claims.claim: Contains polyacetylene with preventative effects against thrombosis and cancer
book_claims.mechanism: Polyacetylene compound found in Apiaceae family (carrots, celery, parsley) displays anti-thrombotic and anti-cancer activity

supplementary_research.finding: Meta-analysis found dietary carrot intake associated with a 26% reduction in gastric cancer risk. Dose-response analysis showed each 100g/day increase reduced risk by 5%.
Falcarinol (a polyacetylene from carrots) reduced precancerous colon lesions by one-third in rats, directly supporting the book's polyacetylene anti-cancer claim.
Beta-carotene from cooked, pureed carrots is 3x more bioavailable than from raw carrots, confirming the book's preparation advice. Adding fat further increased absorption by 50%.
Beta-carotene conversion to vitamin A is tightly regulated by the body, preventing toxicity unlike preformed retinol. Cooking and fat co-ingestion reliably increase carotenoid absorption.

culinary_pairings.source: Fat required for carotenoid absorption; book specifically recommends pairing
Carrot-ginger soup is a classic; both provide anti-inflammatory compounds
Warm spice complements carrot's sweetness in Moroccan salads and soups
Acid balances sweetness; vitamin C enhances iron absorption from carrot greens
Fat from sesame aids carotenoid absorption; complementary flavors in roasted carrot dishes
Same Apiaceae family; foundation of French and Italian cooking bases
Both provide complementary carotenoids and anti-inflammatory compounds

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: 3x more bioavailable cooked and pureed vs raw. Fat co-ingestion essential. Body regulates conversion to vitamin A, preventing toxicity.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: 1-7 mg depending on variety and growing conditions
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Fat-soluble. Reduced precancerous colon lesions by 33% in animal studies. Concentrated in carrot peel -- do not peel if organic.
Converted to vitamin A at roughly half the rate of beta-carotene. Inversely associated with all-cause mortality in NHANES data.
Soluble fiber feeds beneficial gut bacteria. Contributes to the very low glycemic load despite moderate GI.

synergies.description: Fat needed at same meal to optimize carotenoid absorption and conversion to vitamin A
